coredata.dc:description:
The photoelectrochemical (PEC) activity of Strontium titanate (SrTiO3) is constrained by its insufficient light absorption and significant carrier recombination. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) are integrated as co-catalysts onto the surface of oxygen-vacancy-doped mesoporous spherical SrTiO3 nanoparticles to address these limitations and enhance PEC activity. Experimental characterizations and DFT calculations elucidate that the CDs demonstrated significant synergistic enhancements in PEC performance, particularly in band alignment, visible light absorption, and charge carrier transport, which collectively accelerated surface catalytic reactions. Most importantly, the electronic structure of SrTiO3 was optimized by CDs, and their interfacial interaction plays an important role in enhancing its PEC performance. As a result, the optimal 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanode achieves a photocurrent density of 1.37 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, significantly surpassing that of Sr X TiO3
-δ (0.73 mA/cm2) and pristine SrTiO3 (0.109 mA/cm2). Additionally, the incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) reaches 34 % for 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3
-δ , compared to 18.2 % for Sr X TiO3
-δ and 2.6 % for SrTiO3. These findings present a straightforward and effective method for designing high-performance catalysts for PEC water splitting.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots enhance photoelectrochemical water splitting activity of SrTiO3 nanoparticles: Band tuning and excellent charge separation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reveals the comprehensive role of carbon dots as co-catalysts on oxygen-vacancy-doped mesoporous spherical SrTiO3 nanoparticles for significantly enhancing photoelectrochemical OER performance through band tunning and excellent charge transport.

The photoelectrochemical (PEC) activity of Strontium titanate (SrTiO3) is constrained by its insufficient light absorption and significant carrier recombination. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) are integrated as co-catalysts onto the surface of oxygen-vacancy-doped mesoporous spherical SrTiO3 nanoparticles to address these limitations and enhance PEC activity. Experimental characterizations and DFT calculations elucidate that the CDs demonstrated significant synergistic enhancements in PEC performance, particularly in band alignment, visible light absorption, and charge carrier transport, which collectively accelerated surface catalytic reactions. Most importantly, the electronic structure of SrTiO3 was optimized by CDs, and their interfacial interaction plays an important role in enhancing its PEC performance. As a result, the optimal 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanode achieves a photocurrent density of 1.37 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, significantly surpassing that of Sr X TiO3
-δ (0.73 mA/cm2) and pristine SrTiO3 (0.109 mA/cm2). Additionally, the incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) reaches 34 % for 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3
-δ , compared to 18.2 % for Sr X TiO3
-δ and 2.6 % for SrTiO3. These findings present a straightforward and effective method for designing high-performance catalysts for PEC water splitting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen production through photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting based on semiconductors is a promising route to address energy and environmental concerns [1]. Generally, the energy conversion efficiency of PEC water splitting is affected by three factors: light absorption, charge separation [2], and surface catalytic reactions [3]. The light absorption capacity determines the level of solar energy utilization [4]. However, it is challenging to achieve adequate sunlight absorption for most of the currently explored photocatalysts. Moreover, the kinetics of water splitting reaction deeply relies on charge carrier transport and surface reactions at the solid–liquid interface [5]. Unfortunately, although photogenerated electron-hole pairs may possess thermodynamically sufficient potentials for water splitting, they can easily recombine [6,7]. Therefore, overcoming these problems and developing highly active photocatalysts as working electrode materials is of superb significance for PEC water splitting. Strategies for this purpose have been extensively reported, including heterojunction construction [8,9], defect engineering [10], facet engineering [6], surface sensitization [11], etc.

Since the pioneering work by Li et al. in 2010, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention as co-catalysts, owing to their remarkable light absorption, excellent electrical conductivity, low toxicity, and high stability [12–14]. CDs can effectively tune the band structure of semiconductor materials, narrowing their bandgap and extending their light absorption range into the visible spectrum. Additionally, their surface functionalities create active sites for catalytic reactions and facilitate chemical bonding or interfacial interactions with other materials [12]. These attributes make CDs well-suited for photocatalytic and PEC water splitting application. For instance, Lu et al. introduced CDs on the surface of tubular C3N4. CDs can provide active sites and increase the surface temperature of CDs/TCN composite, thereby enhancing the performance of photothermal-assisted photocatalytic water splitting [15]. Song et al. reported a novel CDs-modified amorphous CoP. The coupling interface between CoP and CDs optimized hydrogen/oxygen adsorption energy and accelerated the kinetics of water splitting [16]. Furthermore, Wu et al. constructed a BiVO4/CD/CdS Z-scheme photocatalyst using CDs as solid electronic mediators. CDs expanded the transportation and lifetime of photo-generated charge carriers [17]. The diverse microstructures of CDs such as anisotropic graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with hydrophilic groups, provide a wider array of desirable physicochemical properties for photocatalytic applications[18]. Even through several studies have reported the synergistic effect of CDs when they are incorporated with the host photocatalysts by constructing ternary heterojunctions and defect doping[16,17,19]. There is still a lack of systematic research on the multiple roles of carbon dots in promoting photoelectrocatalytic reactions. Especially when defects are deliberately created on the surface of a host catalyst to regulate the active sites and the catalytic reaction rate, it remains a challenging issue to figure out whether the introduction of CDs under such conditions will bring beneficial effects on the whole PEC reaction.

SrTiO3 is a typical n-type perovskite semiconductor (3.2 eV) with exceptional conductivity, photochemical stability, and low cost, thus widely studied as a promising photoanode material [20–22]. Smartly designing the size, morphology and surface defects of SrTiO3 during the synthesis process can endow it with a higher specific surface area and more active sites, thereby improving its PEC property [6,8]. There are reports confirming that integrating carbon dots on SrTiO3 nanoparticles is an effective approach to significantly enhance its photoelectrocatalytic activity [19,23]. To the best of our knowledge, there are few reports revealing the role of CDs when they are incorporated into the perovskite semiconductors with mesoporous structure and oxygen vacancy defects in PEC water splitting.

Herein, CDs were integrated onto the surface of oxygen vacancy-doped mesoporous SrTiO3 nanospheres for PEC water splitting. Experimental results and DFT calculations demonstrate that the incorporation of CDs induces a synergistic enhancement in the photoelectrocatalytic performance of SrTiO3 by effectively tuning its band structure, facilitating charge carrier migration, and serving as reactive active sites. This synergistic effect enabled the CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ composites to achieve a remarkable maximum incident photon-to-current efficiency (IPCE) of 34 %, significantly exceeding that of pristine SrTiO3. This work presents a simple and effective approach to enhance the PEC activity of perovskite semiconductors by integrating with CDs and reveals the synergistic enhancement effects of CDs within the composites.

Potassium hydroxide (KOH), ethylene glycol ((CH2OH)2), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and ethanol (C2H6O) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., and titanium trichloride (TiCl3) and strontium chloride hexahydrate (SrCl2·6H2O) were purchased from Aladdin Holdings Group Co., Ltd. Each reagent is of analytical grade and was used directly without further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water.

Preparation of SrTiO3 Nanoparticles. In a hydrothermal synthesis process, 0.264 mL of TiCl3 was dropped into 25 mL of deionized water and subjected to agitation for 5 min. Subsequently, 0.8 mL of ethylene glycol was dropwise added and the mixture was stirred for an additional 10 min. Subsequently, 30 mL of a 3 M KOH solution was added, and the mixture was stirred until the color of the solution turned milky white, at which point 0.8 g of SrCl2·6H2O was added into the solution. After a further 30-minute stirring treatment, the as-prepared solution was transferred to a bespoke 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene lined stainless steel high-pressure vessel. Thereafter, the high-pressure vessel is subjected to a thermal treatment at 180 °C for 48 h, followed by a gradual cooling phase. Finally, mesoporous SrTiO3 particles were sonicated and centrifuged (at 4000 rpm for 12 min) three times in 1 M HCl solution, deionized water, and anhydrous ethanol, respectively, and dried at 60°C for 24 h.

Preparation of SrXTiO3-δ Nanoparticles. 1 mmol of as-prepared SrTiO3 powder and 0.5–2 mmol of NaBH4 were mixed in an agate mortar and ground for 30 min. Then the mixture was put into a ceramic boat and heated in a tube furnace under N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. The insulation conditions were set at 300–450 °C and kept for 60 min. After natural cooling, the SrTiO3 samples were washed several times with deionized water and ethanol to remove unreacted NaBH4 and then dried at 60 °C for 24 h. A series of SrTiO3 samples containing oxygen vacancies were finally obtained. The obtained SrTiO3 sample was recorded as Sr X TiO3-
δ X or Sr X TiO3-
δ T (X is the molar ratio of SrTiO3 and NaBH4, δ stands for the amount of oxygen vacancy, and T is the holding temperature).

Preparation of Carbon nanodots. Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized using a standard hydrothermal method. Fallen ginkgo leaves collected from the campus were thoroughly cleaned with ultrapure water and subsequently dried by baking at 60 °C for 48 h. After weighing and crushing, 20 g of the dried ginkgo leaves were dispersed in 60 mL of ultrapure water. This mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. Upon natural cooling, the supernatant was collected, and CDs were obtained through centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 12 min.

Preparation of CDs/SrXTiO3-δ
. In a specific procedure, the 0.01 mg/mL CDs solution was prepared by dispersing CDs powder in deionized water and subjecting the mixture to sonication. To prepare the CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ composite, 100 mg of SrTiO3 nanoparticles and various amounts (from 0.1 mL to 100 mL) of the 0.01 mg/mL CDs solution were mixed in 100 mL of ultrapure water. The resulting mixture was continuously sonicated for three hours while maintaining the ultrasonic temperature below 60 °C. Following sonication, the mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm to collect the precipitate, thus forming the CD/Sr X TiO3-δ composite. By changing the amount of CD solution, the weight ratio of CDs to SrTiO3 was controlled, and this study prepared a series of composites with weight ratios ranging from 0.001 % to 1 %. The resulting sample is designated as X%CDs/Sr X TiO3-δ, where X represents the weight ratio of CDs to Sr X TiO3-δ.

The crystal structures were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Smartlab Rigaku) with Cu Kα (1.5418 Å) radiation at a scanning rate of 10°·min−1 in the 2θ range of 5–90°. The morphology and EDS of the samples were observed by a field-emission scanning electron microscope (ZEISS Ultra Plus) and a transmission electron microscope (Thermo Talos F200X). The spherical aberration-corrected transmission electron microscopy (JEM-ARM200F) was used to characterize the crystalized details of nanostructures. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on a Thermo Scientific TM ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source. All peaks were calibrated with C 1 s peak binding energy at 284.8 eV. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was recorded on an STA 449 F3 Jupiter (Netzsch Instruments, Selb, Germany) coupled to a Mass Spectrometer (MS, Hiden Analytical, model HPR-20). Specimens were placed in Pt crucibles, and TGA-MS data were recorded during heating from ambient temperature to 800 °C (heating rate of 10 °C min−1) under flowing air. The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were recorded with Bruker EMXnano A300 to identify the oxygen vacancy in samples. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were performed based on an FTIR spectrometer (Thermo NEXUS-670) utilizing a KBr pellet as the background to detect the functional groups of SrTiO3. The UV–vis spectra were measured on a Shimadzu UV 3600i plus UV–vis spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) properties were analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLS1000).

The photoelectrochemical test was conducted using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai, China) coupled with a conventional three-electrode cell. For the standard three-electrode electrochemical cell (electrolyte solution, 0.5 M Na2SO4), Pt sheet (2 × 2 cm), Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl), and SrTiO3-based catalysts were applied as the counter electrode, reference electrode, and working electrode, respectively. To prepare SrTiO3 photoelectrode, 5 mg of SrTiO3 powder and 20 μL of naphthol binder were added to a mixed solution of 0.8 mL acetone and 0.2 mL deionized water and sonicated for 5 min. The homogeneous suspension was sprayed onto FTO conductive glass (2 × 2 cm) and dried in a 60 °C drying oven before coating the next layer. A 300 W Xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300C) was used as the solar simulation light source for all PEC tests.

Polarization curves for the OER were obtained using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) at a scan rate of 10 mV·s−1. Photoinduced current–time (I-T) tests were conducted at a bias of 1.23 V versus the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), alternating between light and darkness with 30-second light on/off cycles. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were performed within a frequency range of 100 kHz to 10 mHz while in darkness. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were measured over a potential range of 0.425 to 0.545 V versus RHE. Mott − Schottky plots were recorded at a frequency of 1000 Hz in the dark. Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations were performed using the Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP) [28]. These calculations incorporated a dipole correction and employed the Generalized Gradient Approximation (GGA) with the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) [29] function as the exchange–correlation energy function. The interactions between ionic cores and valence electrons were treated using the projector augmented wave (PAW) pseudopotential method [30,31] based on a plane-wave basis set. The structural and electronic properties of bulk SrTiO3 and carbon dot were calculated using a 2 × 2 × 1 supercell and a 2 × 2 × 3 supercell, respectively. To achieve optimal energy convergence, the k-point sampling was carefully optimized, utilizing a 3 × 3 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack grid for bulk phase calculations of SrTiO3 and CDs calculations. Surface calculations were conducted with a slab model under periodic boundary conditions. The (001) surfaces of both SrTiO3 and CD were selected to construct the heterojunction for computational simulations. A vacuum layer of 20 Å was implemented on all surfaces to effectively mitigate surface effects. During the structural relaxation process, convergence tolerances for energy and forces on each atom were set to less than 10-5 eV and 0.02 eV Å-1, respectively. To ensure result accuracy, a kinetic energy cutoff of 500 eV was applied for the plane waves in all calculations. Structural models were constructed based on experimental X-ray diffraction (XRD) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) data and were generated and visualized using Vesta software. Given the limitations of the GGA in accurately estimating the bandgap and its well-documented shortcomings in addressing d and f electron interactions in transition metals, we employed the GGA + U approach for our electronic structure calculations. Specifically, the GGA + U method was implemented using Dudarev's scheme, with parameters U = 7 and J = 1 specifically applied to Ti-d orbitals [32].

The synthesis diagram of the CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ composite is provided in scheme 1 . First, the mesoporous SrTiO3 nanosphere was synthesized by controlling hydrothermal conditions. Afterward, oxygen vacancies (Ov) were doped on SrTiO3 via a controlled solid-state reaction of NaBH4 and SrTiO3 nanoparticles. Key parameters of the reaction, including the heating temperature and the molar ratio of SrTiO3 to NaBH4, were optimized to identify the Sr X TiO3-
δ samples with the most favorable PEC activity. The transient photocurrent response of a series of Sr X TiO3-
δ samples (figure S1) suggests that the solid-state reaction conditions for achieving the optimal oxygen-vacancy-doped SrTiO3 (Sr X TiO3-
δ ) are that the molar ratio of SrTiO3 to NaBH4 equals 1:1, and the heating temperature is 350 °C, which would be applied for subsequent modification. Afterwards, different amounts of carbon dots (CDs) were loaded onto Sr X TiO3-
δ using an ultrasonic immersion method. We then measured the photocurrent response to determine the optimal CDs concentrations. There are two distinct peaks in photocurrent density that appeared at 0.005 % and 0.1 % when the weight ratio of CDs to Sr X TiO3-δ was tuned from 0 to 1 % (figure S2). Thus, this study focused on the samples of 0.005 % CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ and 0.1 % CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ to investigate the effects of CDs on the PEC activity of Sr X TiO3-
δ at both low and high concentrations.

The surface topography and microstructure of the as-prepared catalysts were characterized using SEM and TEM. The as-prepared pristine SrTiO3, Sr X TiO3-
δ , and CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ samples all exhibit monodispersed distributions (SEM images in Figures S 3a-3c). The pristine SrTiO3 possesses a mesoporous spherical structure, with an average particle size of ∼ 200 nm (SEM images in Figure S 3d). After being doped with Ov and integrated with CDs, the mesoporous spherical structure is preserved (SEM images in Figures S 3e-f), which demonstrates that Ov and CDs have minor effects on the overall appearance of SrTiO3. This unique structure may have a beneficial impact on their PEC activity due to high surface area and roughness. The tested specific surface area of Sr X TiO3-
δ and CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ are 263.96 m2/g and 260.97 m2/g, respectively (Fig. S4), which confirms the conjecture from the SEM images. In meanwhile, it indicates that the doping of a tiny amount of CDs has little effect on the specific surface area of mesoporous Sr X TiO3-
δ .Generally, a larger specific surface area correlates with a higher number of active sites on the catalyst, facilitating the adsorption and conversion of reactants and lowering the energy barrier for catalyst dissociation and adsorption [33,34]. For convenient comparison, Table 1 presents relevant data from existing literature, suggesting that our as-prepared SrTiO3 nanoparticles own the largest BET specific surface area reported since then.

The contrast between the bright and dark areas in the TEM images of the SrTiO3 and Sr X TiO3−
δ spherical nanoparticle (Fig. 1 a and 1b) further confirms its mesoporous structure. As shown in Fig. 1c, nanoparticles approximately less than 10 nm in size are observed on the surface of CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ , moreover, the bright and dark contrast in the particle is decreased, indicating the successful loading of CDs. The HRTEM image of SrTiO3 (Fig. 1d) reveals well-defined lattice fringes across the entire region, demonstrating its exceptional crystallinity. The measured fringe width of 0.276 nm corresponds to the interplanar spacing of SrTiO3 (110) planes. In contrast, the HRTEM image of Sr X TiO3−
δ (Fig. 1e) illustrates an amorphous shell at the edges surrounding the highly crystalline core. This amorphous region is attributed to the surface oxygen vacancy layer, resulting from the use of NaBH4 as an effective oxygen scavenger. In the HRTEM image of CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ (Fig. 1f), lattice fringes with widths of 0.276 [41] and 0.21 nm [42] correspond to the (110) crystal planes of SrTiO3 and (100) crystal planes of CDs, respectively. Fig. S5 provides the measurement process for the lattice fringes of carbon dots within the CDs/SrXTiO3−
δ composite. Therefore, the successful incorporation of CDs into the composite was further confirmed. Notably, the CDs may serve as both light absorption centers and active sites, facilitating the generation of charge carriers and promoting the efficient transfer of local electron-hole pairs. The elemental distribution analysis by EDS mapping (Fig. 1h) reveals the uniform distribution of Sr, Ti, O, and C within the composite, the semicircle with a more obvious distribution of C elements is derived from the TEM carbon support film. The ball aberration corrected high-resolution electron microscopy was used to further identify the exposure of CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ crystal planes. The atomically resolved HAADF-STEM image of CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ is displayed in Fig. 1i. The (100) and (110) crystal planes of SrTiO3 were clearly observed, confirming that SrTiO3 exposed more (001) crystal planes, which is consistent with the previous literature indicating that (001) crystal planes have the lowest formation energy [43].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to investigate the crystal structure of the samples. Compared the XRD patterns (Fig. 2 a) with JCPDS No. 35–0734, the characteristic peaks of pristine SrTiO3, Sr X TiO3-
δ , and CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ all correspond to the (100), (110), (111), (200), (210), (211), (220), (300), and (310) planes as marked, indicating successful fabrication of the cubic phase SrTiO3-based nanocrystals [44]. Notably, doping with oxygen vacancies and incorporating CDs did not result in a significant change in the XRD pattern of the catalysts. However, compared with the pristine SrTiO3, the (110) diffraction peaks of Sr X TiO3-
δ and CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ slightly shifted to smaller diffraction angles with the introduction of oxygen vacancies and carbon dots, indicating there was a slight change in the lattice spacing of SrTiO3 (see Table S1 for specific cell parameters). This phenomenon can be explained by the lattice expansion caused by oxygen vacancy doping [45], as well as the strong interfacial interaction between CDs and Sr X TiO3-
δ [19].

To characterize the existence of oxygen vacancies in Sr X TiO3-
δ , EPR and TGA tests were performed on both SrTiO3 and Sr X TiO3-
δ samples. As depicted in Fig. 2b, the pristine SrTiO3 exhibits no discernible EPR signal; however, Sr X TiO3-
δ displays a pronounced signal at a g-value of 1.97, indicative of paramagnetic oxygen vacancies as reported in previous references [26,46]. This finding confirms the successful incorporation of oxygen vacancies into the SrTiO3 crystal lattice. Fig. 2c presents the TGA results for SrTiO3 and Sr X TiO3-
δ catalysts. The TGA curve of SrTiO3 typically reveals two primary stages of weight loss. The initial weight loss from room temperature to 230 °C is attributed to the evaporation of physically adsorbed water molecules from the surface of SrTiO3. The subsequent weight loss occurs at elevated temperatures from 230 °C to 800 °C and is associated with the removal of chemically adsorbed water and the release of oxygen atoms from the SrTiO3 surface [45,47]. In contrast, Sr X TiO3-
δ exhibits a gentle weight loss trend from room temperature to 800 °C, possibly due to oxygen vacancies being replenished with oxygen during the heating process, thereby mitigating a portion of the observed weight loss.

FTIR spectroscopy is employed to study the impact of CDs on the surface functional groups and optical properties of Sr X TiO3-
δ , as depicted in Fig. 2d. In the Sr X TiO3-
δ particles, broad absorption bands located at approximately 497 cm−1 and 594 cm−1 are correspond to the bending and stretching vibrations of the TiO6 octahedra, respectively [48,49]. After loading with CDs, there is a remarkable enhancement in the characteristic absorption peak related to the vibrational modes of TiO6, which can be attributed to the unique light absorption and emission properties of the carbon dots [12]. Moreover, as the carbon dot loading increases, the intensity of the characteristic peaks of CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ in the FTIR spectrum gradually increased. More importantly, the FTIR spectrum of CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ exhibits new functional groups of O–H, C=O, and COOH [50]. The increase in these functional groups may significantly enhance the hydrophilicity of the composite, thus potentially improving its PEC water splitting performance. The static contact angles of the samples were measured before and after the incorporation of CDs (Fig. 2f). The contact angle of the pristine SrTiO3 was approximately 42.5°, while the contact angle of the CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ composite decreased to 28.66°. This change demonstrates that CDs enhance the hydrophilicity of the composite, which facilitates better interfacial interactions with water and is favorable for enhancing photocatalytic activity [51]. Besides, FTIR analysis of CDs before and after long-term UV exposure was conducted to investigate the structural stability of CDs. As indicated in Fig. 2e, the overall decrease in FTIR peak intensities suggests a potential loss of volatile components or minor unstable species. However, the core functional groups of CDs remain intact, indicating that the essential chemical structure of CDs is preserved under prolonged UV exposure. This structural stability is crucial for maintaining the functionality of CDs in composite materials.

To verify that the CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ heterojunction interface can effectively enhance PEC water splitting, a photoelectrochemical test was first conducted. Fig. 3 a-b presents the LSV curves and transient photocurrent responses (I-T) of SrTiO3-based photoanodes under simulated AM 1.5 G solar radiation (100 mW·cm−2). This performance was assessed in a standard three-electrode system using a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution as the electrolyte. The SrTiO3 photoanode exhibits a notably weak photocurrent response, with a density of only 0.09 mA·cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE. Upon doping SrTiO3 with a suitable amount of oxygen vacancies, the resulting Sr X TiO3-δ photoelectrode reveals a significant enhancement in photocurrent density, reaching 0.73 mA·cm−2. This improvement is attributed to the enhanced charge separation and surface catalytic activity [52]. Furthermore, the loading of CDs on the surface of Sr X TiO3-
δ as light-harvesting centers and co-catalysts leads to an additionally remarkable increase in photocurrent density. Specifically, the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ and 0.1 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanodes achieve photocurrent densities of 1.37 mA·cm−2 and 0.92 mA·cm−2, respectively. Notably, the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanode exhibits the lowest onset potential at 0.65 V vs. RHE, which is 0.25 V lower than that of the original SrTiO3 photoanode. To evaluate catalytic activity and quantify transfer kinetics, the Tafel slopes (Fig. S8) of different samples were calculated to quantify the charge transfer kinetics. The Tafel slope of the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ and 0.1 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ is 273.34 and 463.24 mV dec-1, respectively, which are significantly lower than that of the Sr X TiO3-
δ of 505.97 mV·dec-1. Universally, a smaller Tafel slope represents a higher current density, suggesting faster kinetics of the charge transfer and better catalytic activity.

The incident photon current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of SrTiO3, Sr X TiO3-
δ , and CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanodes was evaluated at 1.23 V vs. RHE (Fig. 3c). Throughout the entire wavelength range, the IPCE values of the Sr X TiO3-
δ and CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanodes are significantly higher than that of the SrTiO3 photoanode, which aligns with the results from the LSV curves. The maximum IPCE of the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanode can reach 34 % at 200 nm, whereas the IPCE of the Sr X TiO3-
δ and SrTiO3 photoanodes are only 18.2 % and 2.6 %, respectively. This indicates that CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanodes exhibit superior light absorption characteristics and charge separation abilities at specific wavelengths. Moreover, the measured IPCE demonstrates wavelength-dependent behavior, consistent with the absorption spectrum of the SrTiO3-based photoanode shown in Fig. 4 a. For convenient comparison with IPCE values of the SrTiO3-based photoanodes reported the related references are listed in Table S2. Additionally, the applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) (Fig. 3d) values of various photoelectrodes were calculated based on the measured J-V curves. The maximum ABPE for the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanode can reach 0.25 % at 0.95 V vs. RHE, which is higher than that of 0.1 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ (0.178 % at 0.96 V vs. RHE), SrXTiO3-δ (0.123 % at 0.97 V vs. RHE), and the original SrTiO3 (0.015 % at 0.98 V vs. RHE). This result suggests that 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanodes possess superior water oxidation catalytic activity and reaction kinetics [2]. Notably, the PEC performance of the 0.1 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanode is slightly inferior to that of the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanode, highlighting the importance of suitable carbon dot concentration in optimizing the overall PEC properties of CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanode.

Outstanding stability is a crucial evaluation index for the practical application of PEC water splitting. As illustrated in Fig. 3e (bottom), the photocurrent density of the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanode was monitored continuously for 10 h under AM 1.5 G irradiation at 1.23 V vs. RHE. The photocurrent initially peaked at 1.44 mA·cm−2 within approximately half an hour and then gradually decreased to 1.39 mA·cm−2 over the next two hours. This slight decline may be attributed to the accumulation of photogenerated holes in the electrode’s space charge region, which could hinder water oxidation kinetics [53]. Following this initial period, a very stable photocurrent density was observed for the remaining duration, indicating excellent photostability and chemical stability of the photoanode during PEC water splitting. To further evaluate the structural integrity of the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanode after 10 h of continuous operation, XRD (Fig. S6) and SEM (Fig. S7) analyses were conducted. As expected, the photoanode exhibited no significant changes in crystal structure and morphology. In addition, the photocurrent response of the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanode was re-evaluated after long-term storage, specifically on the 30th and 120th days (Fig. 3e (top)). The results showed a minor decrease in photocurrent density of approximately 0.05 mA·cm−2 and 0.1 mA·cm−2, respectively. Such reductions may be due to microstructural changes over time. As shown in Fig. 3f, the morphology of the material was characterized after 120 days of storage. The spherical surface of the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ catalyst appeared to exhibit sharper edges and reduced pore size, indicating possible alterations in its morphology.

Besides, the photocatalytic recycling stability tests of CDs/Sr X TiO3
-δ (Fig. S9) showed no performance degradation in 4 cycles, further confirming its excellent reusability.

The optical properties and band alignment of the as-prepared photocatalysts were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4a, the pristine SrTiO3 exhibits an absorption edge around 385 nm, demonstrating a limited visible light absorption intensity. Following the introduction of oxygen vacancies, the absorption edge shifts slightly to 390 nm, with only a negligible increase in visible light absorption intensity. In contrast, the absorption edge of the CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ composite shifts significantly to approximately 480 nm, accompanied by a substantial enhancement in absorption intensity. This finding indicates that the strong light-responsive properties of the CDs markedly improve the visible light absorption capability of the CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ composite. For further comparison, Fig. S10 provides the UV–vis absorption spectrum of both CDs and Sr X TiO3-
δ . The Kubelka-Munk method [15] was employed to estimate the bandgap widths of the samples. As illustrated in the Tauc plots (Fig. 4b), the original SrTiO3 and Sr X TiO3-
δ exhibit a bandgap of around 3.04 eV. In contrast, the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ sample demonstrates a reduced bandgap width of 2.35 eV. Interestingly, as the concentration of CDs increases to 0.1 %, the bandgap width slightly increases to 2.41 eV. This notable decrease in bandgap can be attributed to the quantum confinement effect of the CDs [54].

To further investigate the effect of CDs on the band structure of Sr X TiO3-
δ , we assessed the valence band (VB) positions of the photocatalysts using XPS analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, the VB of the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ sample shifts positively from 1.89 eV to 2.23 eV, indicating a significant change in its electronic structure. By using the measured VB position and bandgap, we calculated the conduction band (CB) positions using the following formula, thereby constructing the energy band diagram of the catalyst.
(7)
EVB = ECB + Eg
Fig. 4d confirms that both SrTiO3 and CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ fulfil the thermodynamic conditions for PEC oxygen evolution reactions. However, the more positive VB of the CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ composite results in a sufficiently hole-rich region, thereby enhancing the thermodynamic driving force for the PEC water splitting reaction. In conclusion, the electronic band structure of the SrTiO3-based photocatalyst can be effectively tuned through the integration of CDs [12,13], with the extent of change correlating to the concentration of CDs used. Consequently, both light absorption capacity and photocarrier generation are enhanced, contributing to optimized PEC performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interfacial interaction and transport route of photogenerated carrier
ce:section.ce:para:
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted to investigate the impact of oxygen vacancies and CDs on surface electron transfer in Sr X TiO3-
δ . As shown in Fig. 5 a, the XPS survey spectra exhibit characteristic peaks corresponding to O 1 s, Ti 2p, C 1 s, and Sr 3d. The high-resolution Sr 3d XPS spectrum of SrTiO3 (Fig. 5b) displays two peaks at 134.58 eV and 132.8 eV, corresponding to Sr 3d3/2 and Sr 3d5/2 of Sr2+ [55], respectively. Upon introducing oxygen vacancies, the Sr 3d peaks in Sr X TiO3-
δ shift to lower binding energies observed at 134.36 eV and 132.31 eV. Conversely, the incorporation of CDs results in shifts toward higher binding energies, with values of 134.72 eV and 132.96 eV for the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ sample, and 134.59 eV and 132.85 eV for the 0.1 %CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ sample. Fig. 5c presents the high-resolution XPS spectra for Ti 2p, where the two asymmetric peaks correspond to Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 [44,55]. In Fig. 5d, the O 1 s spectrum is clearly resolved into three components: lattice oxygen (Olat), oxygen vacancy (Ov) and adsorbed oxygen (Oads) [44]. We calculated the proportion of the Ov area in the O XPS spectra (Fig. S11) to semi quantitatively assess the changes in oxygen vacancy content among different samples. The results indicate that NaBH4 effectively reduced a fraction of the oxygen atoms, increasing the Ov area from 15.23 % in SrTiO3 to 23.94 % in Sr X TiO3-
δ . In contrast, the addition of CDs did not affect the oxygen vacancy content. Specifically, the samples of 0.005 %CD/Sr X TiO3-
δ and 0.1 %/CD/Sr X TiO3-
δ exhibited Ov area proportions of 23.42 % and 24.01 %, respectively. The displacement trends observed in the Ti 2p and O 1 s peaks are consistent with those of the Sr 3d peak, and the specific positions are available in Fig. 5c-d. The shifts of characteristic peaks indicate that oxygen vacancies and CDs altered the equilibrium electron density of SrTiO3 [15,45] and confirm the interfacial interactions within the CD/Sr X TiO3-
δ heterojunctions.

Variations in binding energy suggest electron migration across the interface. A shift to higher binding energy indicates electron exportation, whereas a shift to lower binding energy suggests electron importation [56,57]. Combined with the aforementioned XPS results, we conclude that oxygen vacancies serve as electron-trapping centers [26], facilitating electron migration within the SrTiO3 surface. Furthermore, the introduction of CDs results in a loss of intrinsic charges from SrTiO3, while the CDs gain the corresponding intrinsic electron. The observed effects of CDs on surface electron transfer in SrTiO3 are consistent with existing literature, suggesting a tendency for charge transfer from the SrTiO3 surface to the CDs [51]. This charge transfer may enhance the separation of photogenerated charge carriers and promote the participation of photogenerated holes in the OER on the surface of SrTiO3.

To gain deeper insights into the behavior of charge carriers and the impact of interface charge separation on PEC performance, Mott-Schottky (MS) curves were collected on the pristine SrTiO3, Sr X TiO3−
δ , and CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ photoanodes. As presented in Fig. 6 a, the positive slopes of the MS curves for all photoanodes indicate their n-type semiconductor characteristics. Notably, after doping with oxygen vacancies, the flat band potential of Sr X TiO3−
δ shifts from −0.483 V (vs. NHE) to −0.435 V. Upon loading CDs, this potential experienced a further positive shift; specifically, the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ reaches 0.175 V, while the 0.1 %CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ shifts to 0.067 V. These shifts align with the observed alterations in conduction band locations. Furthermore, the slopes of the MS curves for the CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ photoanodes are considerably smaller than those of the SrTiO3 and Sr X TiO3−
δ photoanode, which confirms a significant increase in charge carrier density (ND). The carrier density is calculated based on the corresponding slopes using Equation (1–2) and depicted in Fig. 6b. These results indicate that the ND of the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ composite is 2.7 and 3.4 times higher than that of Sr X TiO3−
δ and SrTiO3, respectively. This enhancement in ND is attributed to the increased donor density [25,58], which substantially facilitates charge separation and transport efficiency, thereby enhancing the OER activity.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy is a potent method for investigating the surface recombination in photo-generated electron-hole pairs. Emission intensity in PL spectra is correlated with non-radiative recombination and lower intensities signify less recombination [59]. As illustrated in Fig. 6c, the PL spectra of SrTiO3, Sr X TiO3−
δ , and CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ photoanodes were recorded under excitation at 390 nm. The data reveal that SrTiO3 exhibits the highest peak intensity, while Sr X TiO3−
δ shows a modest reduction in emission intensity. In particular, the CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ photoanode displays a pronounced decline in emission peak intensity, with the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ registering the lowest intensity among all samples. This observation underscores that the incorporation of CDs remarkedly facilitates the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby enhancing the overall reaction efficiency of the photoanode materials. To track carrier dynamics, time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay spectra were obtained. As presented in Fig. 6d, the average charge lifetimes of SrTiO3, S X TiO3−
δ , 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ , and 0.1 %CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ photoanodes are 1.23, 1.34, 1.816, and 1.591 ns, respectively. Namely, the incorporation of CDs effectively prolongs the carrier lifetimes of Sr X TiO3−
δ , implying an improved separation and retention of photogenerated carriers.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to investigate the charge transfer characteristics of the photoelectrodes. The corresponding Randle equivalent circuit consists of Rs (electrolyte solution resistance), Rct (interface charge transfer resistance), and CPE (constant phase element) (Fig. 6e). The Nyquist plot reveals that the 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ photoelectrode exhibits the smallest semicircle radius, indicating the lowest interface charge transfer resistance and the fastest charge transfer rate [60]. The fitted resistance values for all photoelectrodes are listed in Table S1. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests (Fig. S12) were conducted to evaluate the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) and subsequently compare the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) of the photoanodes. Fig. 6f displays the relationship between the current density difference and scan rate in the non-Faradaic reaction region, along with Cdl calculated from the slope. The obtained Cdl values for 0.005 %CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ and 0.1 %CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ are 553.7 and 539.4 μF·cm−2, respectively, significantly outperforming Sr X TiO3−
δ (443 μF·cm−2) and SrTiO3 (318.4 μF·cm−2). A larger Cdl value indicates an enhanced ECSA, suggesting that CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ possess more active sites for photoelectrochemical OER.

DFT calculations were conducted to further elucidate the enhancement in PEC property of CDs/SrXTiO3−
δ . The optimized models of SrTiO3, SrXTiO3−
δ , and CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ are presented in Fig. 7 a-c. As exhibited in Fig. 7d, the DOS analysis illustrates that the conduction band edge of SrTiO3 primarily consists of unoccupied Ti 3d orbitals, while the valence band edge is mainly composed of filled O 2p states. These results align with previous studies [61]. In contrast to the pristine SrTiO3, Sr X TiO3−
δ and CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ display a continuous state that crosses the Fermi level, indicating that the incorporation of Ov and CDs significantly improves the conductivity. This enhancement can facilitate efficient charge transfer during catalytic reactions [62]. The shift of the Fermi level towards the valence band edge results from the formation of Ti3+ ions associated with oxygen vacancies [32]. Additionally, the band gaps of SrTiO3, Sr X TiO3−
δ , and CDs/Sr X TiO3−
δ were evaluated using the DOS data. The bandgap values are generally underestimated due to the limitations of the Generalized Gradient Approximation (GGA), which often inadequately predicts the Coulomb interactions of d and f electrons. Therefore, we employed a modified DFT + U-method [63] and the calculated bandgap values were found to be 2.13 eV, 1.64 eV, and 1.01 eV, respectively. These results further confirm that carbon dots successfully modulate the energy band structure of Sr X TiO3−
δ . In summary, oxygen vacancies donate additional electrons to the conduction band, increasing charge carrier concentration, while CDs introduce mid-gap states that optimize band alignment and enhance light absorption. The synergistic effect of Ov and CDs leads to a more pronounced improvement in conductivity compared to their separate contributions.

The free electron distribution of SrTiO3 (Fig. 7e) and Sr X TiO3-
δ (Fig. 7f) indicates that the electron density at oxygen vacancies is much higher than that at oxygen atoms, which implies Ov may become trapping centers for photo-generated holes and promote OER. Fig. 7g illustrates the charge density differences for the CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ heterojunction, where the electrons are predominantly localized around the CDs. The CDs (001)/Sr X TiO3-
δ (001) interface model was constructed based on the optimized (001) slab models for SrTiO3 and CDs, ensuring a lattice mismatch of less than 5 %. Obviously, the interaction at the CDs and Sr X TiO3-
δ interface induces charge redistribution and transfer, resulting in electron depletion on the Sr X TiO3-
δ surface and electron accumulation on the CDs [51]. These observations are consistent with the findings from XPS analysis, providing strong evidence for the transfer of electrons from Sr X TiO3-
δ to CDs across the interface. Fig. 7h and Fig. 7i present the charge density distribution of carbon dots after coupling with Sr X TiO3−
δ . The disparity in positive and negative electrons on the surface of carbon dots plays a crucial role in suppressing carrier recombination at the interface, thereby further enhancing PEC catalytic reactions.

Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spin-trapping experiments with dimethylpyridine nitrogen oxide (DMPO) as a spin-trapping reagent were conducted to further investigate the charge transfer mechanism in the CDs/Sr X TiO3
-δ system. As shown in Fig. 8 b, after 5 min of light irradiation, distinct characteristic peaks corresponding to DMPO-·O2 – adducts were observed. For Sr X TiO3
-δ , a significantly enhanced ·O2 – signal was detected, confirming that oxygen vacancies act as efficient charge-trapping sites [64]. These vacancies capture photo-generated electrons, which subsequently react with adsorbed O2 molecules to form superoxide radicals (·O2 –). In contrast, the ·O2 – signal was markedly reduced in CDs/SrXTiO3-δ, indicating that CDs introduce additional charge-trapping sites, capturing electrons that would otherwise participate in ·O2 – formation. Furthermore, CDs facilitate direct electron transfer from Sr X TiO3-
δ to the surface, where electrons are utilized in other reduction processes rather than generating ·O2 – [65]. This observation is consistent with the charge density differences calculated by DFT, which show electron accumulation on CDs and depletion on Sr X TiO3-
δ (Fig. 7g).

The practical applicability and cost-effectiveness of the CDs/SrXTiO3-δ catalyst were systematically evaluated. As depicted in Fig. S13, the catalyst demonstrates exceptional performance across a broad pH range (acidic to alkaline), with optimal activity in alkaline conditions due to accelerated oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics, as evidenced by Tafel slope analysis. Moreover, the catalyst exhibits enhanced photocurrent response under high light intensity (Fig. S14), confirming its efficient solar energy utilization. The PEC performance was further assessed in real water conditions (Fig. S15), revealing that the catalyst’s performance in tap water closely matches that in laboratory settings (0.5 M Na2SO4), underscoring its stability in natural environments. In wastewater, while a minor performance decline occurs at potentials above 1.8 V vs. RHE, the catalyst maintains stability at lower potentials, demonstrating its adaptability to complex water matrices. The synthesis cost, including raw materials and energy consumption, was calculated to be economically feasible (Table S4). In summary, despite challenges in scalability and industrial viability, the catalyst’s wide pH tolerance, light intensity adaptability, and superior performance in real water conditions emphasize its potential for large-scale applications.

This work integrated an appropriate amount of carbon dots onto oxygen-vacancy-doped mesoporous spherical SrTiO3 to enhance its photoelectrochemical water splitting activity. A series of characterizations and calculations explain the possible mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic performance. The high specific surface area of composites provides a larger number of reactive sites, while the moderate amount of surface oxygen vacancies act as capture centers for photo-generated holes, facilitating surface charge migration. More importantly, the introduction of CDs effectively tuned the band structure of Sr X TiO3-
δ and significantly enhanced its visible light absorption ability. Concurrently, the heterojunction induced intrinsic charge transfer from the surface of Sr X TiO3-
δ to CDs, greatly promoting the separation and transport of photogenerated carriers. Based on the synergistic enhancement effects, the optimal CDs/Sr X TiO3-
δ photoanode demonstrated highly active PEC water splitting under full spectrum irradiation. This work provides a new perspective on utilizing CDs to modify semiconductors and reveals the synergistic enhancement effect of CDs, offering a promising route for designing low-cost and efficient PEC materials with good stability.

Haitao Fu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Xizhong An: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Dawei Su: Writing – review & editing, Software, Resources.
